topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The solicitor’s crisp voice echoed in the cavernous drawing-room, detailing the division of Grandmother Elara’s estate. Liam stood by the tall, dusty window, his jaw tight enough to crack bone. He watched his sister, Clara, casually lean against the mahogany mantelpiece, a picture of practiced nonchalance. When the lawyer finally announced that the entire house, including the ancestral home, was to be split equally, Liam felt a sudden, sharp tightening in his chest. He turned, his gaze locking onto Clara’s smirking expression. “Equally?” he managed, his voice dangerously level. “After everything she built? You think you deserve half of this?” He didn't wait for a reply, turning abruptly and striding towards the grand staircase, the sound of his boots heavy and deliberate on the marble floor.

[story 2]

"Honestly, Liam, don't make such a fuss," Clara said, smoothing down the silk of her dress as if dismissing a bothersome fly. She surveyed the opulent, slightly decaying rooms with an air of ownership, a subtle arch to her eyebrow signaling superiority. When the will was read, confirming the equal split, a flush crept up Liam’s neck. He clenched his fists inside his pockets, the fabric digging into his palms. "It’s not about the money, Clara," he stated, his tone clipped and brittle. "It’s about respect. You always got the easier path, the attention. This house… it feels like you’re taking what’s rightfully mine just because you’re… well, you’re *you*." He took a deep, shaky breath, trying to keep the tremor out of his voice as he walked towards the library, his shoulders rigid.

[story 3]

I watched Clara accept the documents with a languid grace that grated on my nerves. She didn't even look at me, just offered a thin, condescending smile. When the lawyer finished detailing the inheritance, I felt a strange, hot pressure behind my eyes. I couldn't help but let my gaze linger on the way she subtly positioned herself—relaxed, entitled—in the center of the room. "You really think this is fair?" I asked, my voice surprisingly soft, almost dangerously quiet. "After all the years I spent looking after her, the things I sacrificed? You just expect a slice of the pie because you’re the favored one?" A muscle jumped in my cheek. I turned away, focusing intently on a chipped piece of porcelain on a side table, refusing to meet her eyes.
